text: This is the bronchoscopic view of the right upper lobe takeoff showing considerable extrinsic impingement.
We divide the intercostals under thoroscopic guidance because the skin incision is too limiting to do it through a traditional approach.
We start working at the hilum.
However, because of the post-obstructive pneumonia,
there's significant involvement.

And so we work posteriorly now with the camera from the posterior port, dissecting out the right upper lobe bronchus and the right main stem bronchus, as well as azagus in order to divide this and expose the distal trachea.
We're using a three millimeter hook as a pediatric instrument.
The smaller the instruments, the greater opportunity you have to put more instruments in the small spaces that we use.
So we're dissecting underneath the azagus in order to get this off of the right main stem.
And now that the azagus has been dissected with the suction there, it can be divided with an echelon stapler.
Now we're working back medially where there's some involvement of the cava.
Everything is fibrosed and scarred in.
It's unclear what's involved, but it doesn't appear to be cancer unless we're directly dissecting this.
And we'll eventually staple it off just to make sure we have no bleeding.
The first firing of the stapler has been done and here's a second firing completing the dissection of the asagus away from the cava and into the mediastinum.
This will then allow us to reflect the lobe.
Here we're taking the anterior branch of the vein.
I typically don't like to divide the entire vein because I feel it gets the lobe congested and makes it more challenging to work with.
And so here we'll just take this branch
then expose the nodes and the truncus arteriosus.
We reflect the lung medially and start working posteriorly to get around the right main stem and place an umbilical tape here.
And then back again working anteriorly, the truncus has been divided and this is the recurrent branch.
These smaller branches can be taken with the ligature
or with sutures, as you see in the foreground.
And now you can see that the right main stem is elevated, and actually we're working on the subcarinal space from an anterior approach.
This is more commonly done on the left side, but we believe it's important to do a complete lymphadenectomy and don't think that it compromises our anastomosis.
And so now we have our umbilical tapes around the right main stem as well as the bronchus intermedius, and one can identify the right upper lobe takeoff.
We're going to take a knife and transect the right mean stem bronchus along the plane of dissection.
We typically use a knife for the first cut, and then we'll use scissors to complete our transection as it's a bit more controlled.
And so here the stem bleeding is being controlled with the ligature, which we use frequently for this type of problem.
And then now the bronchus intermedius is being divided.
the remaining membranous bronchus.

So now our lobe is completely divided, and we're ready to do our anastomosis.
but we need our margins.

And then now we've put the camera posteriorly and we're waiting on our margins.
During this period, since it's a significant amount of time, we typically harvest the thymic flap or pericardial fat pad and the thymic flap.
In this operation, we actually did not have a good blood supply to the fat pad and thus aborted

fat pad and went for an intercostal muscle as pulling the fat pad under the rest of the thymus wasn't an adequate amount of tissue and thus we use the camera to shear down the muscle off of the rib as seen here with the madsen elevator you can just strip it down with minimal amount of trauma
And you can see it's a healthy muscle.
We've not used a rigid rib retractor.
And so here's the anastomosis.
We run these all the time and have not had a problem.
However, during the operation, the anastomosis, we will interrupt and use two or three sutures instead of just one so that we don't purse string the suture.
And here's an example of that.
We use a knot pusher and then complete the anastomosis.
There was a bit of a size discrepancy here, but we just make up for it with the suturing technique.
Now the camera's back anteriorly and we're bringing our muscle flap around our anastomosis.
Although some have reported calcification of this, if it's well vascularized, we have not experienced this as a problem.
So we're tacking our muscle flap to the anastomosis and to itself.
And we will also use some of the thymic flap that we harvested.
